title: A Universal Space of Brain Dynamics for Unveiling Cognitive Transitions and Individual Differences
summary: Representing dynamical systems through data-driven universal spaces has proven effective; however, achieving this universality for human brain activity remains a significant challenge, further aggravated by diverse cognitive states and individual subjects. Recognizing that spatial properties reflect physical wiring while temporal properties reflect brain function, we develop Universal Brain Dynamics (UBD) to construct a universal space tailored to brain activity and quantify corresponding dynamics using a model-derived Jacobian matrix. Crucially, we validate UBD's universality by accurately predicting functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) signals (Pearson's r > 0.9) across eight states and 963 subjects in the Human Connectome Project (HCP). Through evaluating resting-state fMRI represented within UBD, we gain insight into how infra-slow fluctuation (ISF) underpins brain activity. Furthermore, we reveal a new perspective on structure-function coupling (SFC) by analyzing the temporal sequence of brain dynamics. Extending UBD to task-evoked states, we derive brain dynamics across various cognitive conditions, elucidating the neural mechanisms driving cognitive transitions at a finer granularity. For individual differences, we compare brain dynamics across subjects to identify the neural underpinnings of these variations. Our findings suggest that synergistically integrating spatial and temporal properties of brain activity establishes a universal space for its unfolding, enabling the precise numerical analysis of underlying neural mechanisms across varying conditions.

AI.tldr: UBD constructs a universal dynamical space for brain activity using spatial (wiring) and temporal (function) properties, achieving high-accuracy fMRI prediction (r>0.9) across 8 states and 963 subjects, enabling analysis of infra-slow fluctuations, structure-function coupling, cognitive transitions, and individual differences.
AI.motivation: Existing universal spaces work for dynamical systems but extending this to human brain activity is challenged by diverse cognitive states and individual subjects. The authors aim to build a truly universal space that integrates both spatial and temporal properties of brain activity.
AI.method: UBD (Universal Brain Dynamics) constructs a universal space using spatial properties (physical wiring) and temporal properties (brain function), quantified via a model-derived Jacobian matrix. Validated on HCP data with 963 subjects across 8 states, predicting fMRI signals.
AI.result: Achieved Pearson's r > 0.9 for fMRI prediction across eight states and 963 subjects. Revealed infra-slow fluctuation (ISF) underpinnings, new structure-function coupling (SFC) perspectives, and finer-grained neural mechanisms of cognitive transitions and individual differences.
AI.conclusion: Synergistically integrating spatial and temporal properties of brain activity can establish a universal space for its unfolding, enabling precise numerical analysis of underlying neural mechanisms across varying conditions.

title: DynoSys: A Dynamic Systems Framework for Multimodal Integration of Genetic, Environmental, and Neurobiological Signals
summary: Understanding the development of adolescent behavioral and mental health outcomes requires integrating genetic predisposition, environmental exposures, and neurobiological processes over time. Here, we present a unified quantitative framework that models the human body as a dynamic system, where genetic factors form the foundational state, environmental exposures act as time-varying inputs, the brain might serve as a mediation processor, and behavioral phenotypes emerge as system outputs. Using longitudinal data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, we construct harmonized multi-domain representations across six phenotypes: externalizing behavior, internalizing behavior, and four substance use initiation outcomes (alcohol, nicotine, cannabis, and any substance use). We integrate polygenic risk scores (PRS), multi-domain environmental features, and multimodal neuroimaging representations derived through stability selection and dimensionality reduction. Our framework supports both continuous longitudinal modeling and survival-based event modeling through a unified data structure. We further develop interpretable domain-level representations using principal components, weighted risk scores, and cluster-based summaries. These representations enable downstream modeling using survival analysis, state-space models, and machine learning approaches. This work establishes a scalable and interpretable framework for studying how genetic and environmental factors interact over time to shape behavioral outcomes, providing a foundation for identifying modifiable risk factors and informing early intervention strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a unified quantitative framework integrating genetic predisposition, environmental exposures, and neuroimaging data to model adolescent behavioral and mental health outcomes, demonstrated on ABCD Study data across six phenotypes.
AI.motivation: To address the need for an integrative approach that combines genetic, environmental, and neurobiological factors over time to better understand adolescent behavioral and mental health development.
AI.method: The framework models the body as a dynamic system: genetic factors as foundational state, environmental exposures as time-varying inputs, brain as mediation processor, and behaviors as outputs. Uses ABCD Study data with PRS, multi-domain environmental features, and multimodal neuroimaging via stability selection and dimensionality reduction. Supports continuous longitudinal modeling and survival-based event modeling.
AI.result: Constructed harmonized multi-domain representations across six phenotypes (externalizing, internalizing, and four substance use outcomes). Developed interpretable domain-level representations using principal components, weighted risk scores, and cluster-based summaries for downstream survival analysis, state-space models, and machine learning.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a scalable and interpretable framework for studying genetic-environmental interactions over time shaping behavioral outcomes, with potential for identifying modifiable risk factors and informing early intervention strategies.

title: EFGPP: Exploratory framework for genotype-phenotype prediction
summary: Predicting complex human traits from genetic data is challenging because different genetic, clinical, and molecular data sources often contain different parts of the signal. Here, we present EFGPP, a reproducible framework for generating, ranking, and combining multiple types of data for genotype-to-phenotype prediction. We applied EFGPP to migraine prediction using UK Biobank data from 733 individuals. The framework combined genotype-derived features, principal components, clinical and metabolomic covariates, and polygenic risk scores generated from migraine and depression GWAS using PLINK, PRSice-2, AnnoPred, and LDAK-GWAS. The best single data type achieved a test AUC of 0.644, while combining multiple data types improved performance to 0.688 using migraine-focused inputs and 0.663 using cross-trait depression-derived inputs. Genetic features alone did not outperform the covariates-only baseline, but genotype-derived features performed better than PRS alone, and depression-derived PRS showed useful predictive signal. Overall, EFGPP provides a practical proof-of-concept framework for prioritising and integrating heterogeneous genetic data sources for complex phenotype prediction.

AI.tldr: EFGPP is a framework for integrating diverse genetic, clinical, and molecular data to predict complex traits like migraine, achieving improved AUC (0.688) over single data types (0.644) in UK Biobank data.
AI.motivation: Predicting complex human traits from genetic data is challenging because different data sources contain different parts of the signal, and no single data type captures the full picture.
AI.method: The EFGPP framework generates, ranks, and combines multiple data types including genotype-derived features, principal components, clinical/metabolomic covariates, and polygenic risk scores from migraine and depression GWAS (using PLINK, PRSice-2, AnnoPred, LDAK-GWAS), applied to UK Biobank migraine data (N=733).
AI.result: Best single data type AUC=0.644; combined migraine-focused inputs AUC=0.688; cross-trait depression inputs AUC=0.663; genetic features alone didn't outperform covariates baseline; genotype-derived features outperformed PRS alone.
AI.conclusion: EFGPP provides a practical proof-of-concept framework for prioritizing and integrating heterogeneous genetic data sources for complex phenotype prediction.

title: Equilibrium and Competition in Evolutionary Dynamics
summary:
A fundamental problem in protobiological dynamics is to understand how chemically generated polymers can form persistent sequence distributions before the emergence of replication. We study deterministic polymer growth in which each finite sequence is followed along its genealogical structure. The system pictures an open polymerization cascade in which each polymer is produced from a unique precursor and lost by degradation and further extension. Setting fixed activated precursors, we show global well-posedness, positivity, uniqueness of a strictly positive equilibrium, and exponential convergence to an explicit steady state distribution. Under an additional uniform decay condition, this convergence becomes global exponential stability in a uniform norm.
  We then couple the polymerization to a shared environmental resource with logistic growth and depletion by two activated precursors. In the resulting binary polymerization competition model, the equilibrium structure is governed by a three-dimensional core subsystem. We prove that strictly positive equilibria exist exactly above a sharp resource threshold. At the threshold the equilibrium is unique, while above it two positive branches appear. The lower branch is unstable and the upper branch is locally stable. For the complete infinite system, we exhibit positivity, global componentwise existence, a priori bounds, and under persistence and dominance assumptions, global exponential stability.
  Finally, we introduce template directed replication through a replicator term. The pre-replicative equilibrium continues only under neutral fitness, and heterogeneous fitness removes it as an equilibrium of the replicated system.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes deterministic polymer growth models in protobiology, proving well-posedness, existence of unique positive equilibria, and exponential convergence. It extends to binary polymerization competition with resource constraints and template-directed replication, revealing threshold-dependent equilibria and stability properties.
AI.motivation: To understand how chemically generated polymers can form persistent sequence distributions before the emergence of replication, addressing a fundamental gap in protobiological dynamics.
AI.method: Deterministic polymer growth models with genealogical structure, open polymerization cascades, logistic resource coupling, and template-directed replication terms. Analyzed via equilibrium theory, stability analysis, and convergence proofs.
AI.result: Global well-posedness, positivity, uniqueness of strictly positive equilibria, and exponential convergence to steady state. Binary competition reveals sharp resource threshold with two positive branches (unstable lower, stable upper). Template replication destroys pre-replicative equilibrium unless fitness is neutral.
AI.conclusion: Rigorous mathematical foundations for pre-replicative polymer sequence persistence are established, with implications for understanding the transition from chemical to evolutionary dynamics in protobiology.

title: Epistatic strength, modularity, and locus heterogeneity shape the number of local optima in fitness landscapes
summary: Fitness landscapes provide a quantitative framework for understanding how natural selection shapes evolutionary trajectories. A central feature of these landscapes is their number of local optima, which determines whether fitness-increasing evolution can proceed towards a global optimum or become trapped on suboptimal peaks. Although multiple peaks are known to require reciprocal sign epistasis, the quantitative relationship between epistasis and number of peaks remains incompletely understood. Here, we show that for a broad class of unstructured fitness landscapes, i.e. isotropic Gaussian random fields, the expected number of local optima is determined by a single local measure of epistasis: the correlation of fitness effects. This provides a baseline prediction for the number of peaks in typical unstructured landscapes and links peak density directly to the amount of reciprocal sign epistasis. This baseline changes when epistatic interactions are structured. We show that clustering interactions within blocks of loci slightly increases the number of local optima. In contrast, strong heterogeneity between loci, where only a small subset of loci participate in epistatic interactions, causes the number of peaks to collapse. These results show that the number of local optima is governed not only by the overall strength of epistasis, but also by how epistatic interactions are distributed across the genotype space. Our framework therefore reconciles the central role of reciprocal sign epistasis with the observation that landscapes with similar amounts of epistasis can differ substantially in ruggedness, and provides a guide to the range of peak numbers expected in typical landscapes.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that in isotropic Gaussian random fields, the expected number of local optima is determined by a single local measure of epistasis—correlation of fitness effects—and that structuring epistatic interactions (clustering vs. heterogeneity) significantly alters peak density, reconciling why landscapes with similar epistasis can differ in ruggedness.
AI.motivation: Understanding the quantitative relationship between epistasis and the number of local optima in fitness landscapes, which determines whether evolution can reach global optima or gets trapped on suboptimal peaks.
AI.method: Analytical derivation for isotropic Gaussian random fields linking expected number of local optima to correlation of fitness effects; analysis of structured epistasis via block clustering and locus heterogeneity.
AI.result: (1) Expected number of peaks is determined by a single local measure of epistasis. (2) Clustering interactions slightly increases peak number. (3) Strong locus heterogeneity (few loci participating in epistasis) collapses the number of peaks.
AI.conclusion: The number of local optima depends not only on overall epistasis strength but also on how interactions are distributed across the genotype space, explaining differences in ruggedness among similarly epistatic landscapes.

title: A temperature-driven diffusion model of Usutu virus spread in Germany with spillover into neighbouring countries
summary: Usutu virus (USUV) is a flavivirus of the Japanese encephalitis complex transmitted between \textit{Culex} mosquitoes and birds, a transmission pattern similar to that of the West Nile virus (WNV). In Germany, the first case of USUV was detected in 2010 in mosquitoes collected in the town of Weinheim, and by 2018 the virus had spread to almost the entire country. Interestingly, the infection front exhibited a clockwise rotational spread pattern throughout the years, a pattern completely different from that of the WNV. This clockwise progression corresponded closely with the spatial temperature gradient, suggesting that warmer regions probably facilitated faster viral amplification and onward transmission. Understanding the drivers that influence the spreading patterns of arboviruses is important as it guides surveillance and implementation of control strategies. In this study, we develop a reaction-diffusion partial differential equation (PDE) model to investigate the spatial spread of USUV in Germany within an extended domain that includes some neighbouring countries (Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg), thereby capturing cross-border transmission processes. Mosquito parameters, i.e., extrinsic incubation rate, mortality and biting rates, are temperature-driven, as temperature plays an important role in the activity of mosquitoes. Our model qualitatively reproduced the main spatial trends of USUV in Germany and surrounding countries. The heterogeneous spread pattern arises from the interplay of diffusion and spatially varying temperature, which together may influence determine regions with higher transmission potential.

AI.tldr: This study develops a reaction-diffusion PDE model to investigate the spatial spread of USUV in Germany and neighboring countries, finding that heterogeneous temperature gradients and diffusion drive the clockwise rotational spread pattern of the virus.
AI.motivation: Understanding drivers behind the spatial spread patterns of arboviruses like USUV is important for guiding surveillance and implementing effective control strategies.
AI.method: A reaction-diffusion partial differential equation (PDE) model was developed incorporating temperature-driven mosquito parameters (extrinsic incubation rate, mortality, and biting rates) to simulate USUV spread across Germany and neighboring countries.
AI.result: The model qualitatively reproduced the main spatial trends of USUV, showing that the heterogeneous (clockwise rotational) spread pattern arises from the interplay of diffusion and spatially varying temperature.
AI.conclusion: Temperature gradients and diffusion together influence regions with higher transmission potential and explain the unique clockwise spread pattern of USUV in Germany, which differs from WNV.

title: NeuralSet: A High-Performing Python Package for Neuro-AI
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly central to understanding how the brain processes information. However, the integration of neuroscience and modern AI is bottlenecked by a fragmented software ecosystem. Current tools are siloed by recording modality and optimized for small-scale, in-memory workflows, limiting the use of massive, naturalistic datasets. Here, we introduce NeuralSet, a Python framework that efficiently unifies the processing of diverse neural recordings (including fMRI, M/EEG, and spikes) and complex experimental stimuli (such as text, audio, and video). By decoupling experimental metadata from lazy, memory-efficient data extraction, NeuralSet harmonizes standard neuroscientific preprocessing pipelines with pretrained deep learning embeddings. This approach provides a single PyTorch-ready interface that scales seamlessly from local prototyping to high-performance cluster execution. By eliminating manual data wrangling and ensuring full computational provenance, NeuralSet establishes a scalable, unified infrastructure for the next generation of neuro-AI research.

AI.tldr: NeuralSet is a Python framework that unifies processing of diverse neural recordings (fMRI, M/EEG, spikes) with complex stimuli, providing a scalable PyTorch-ready interface from local to cluster computing.
AI.motivation: Current neuro-AI integration is bottlenecked by fragmented, siloed software tools that are optimized for small-scale workflows and cannot handle massive, naturalistic datasets.
AI.method: NeuralSet decouples experimental metadata from lazy, memory-efficient data extraction, harmonizing standard preprocessing pipelines with pretrained deep learning embeddings into a single PyTorch-ready interface.
AI.result: The framework scales seamlessly from local prototyping to high-performance cluster execution, eliminating manual data wrangling and ensuring full computational provenance.
AI.conclusion: NeuralSet establishes a scalable, unified infrastructure for next-generation neuro-AI research.

title: Donor-Aware scRNA-seq Benchmarks for IBD Classification
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Independent study at Oklahoma Christian University, advised by Fang Li. Code: https://github.com/Jonathan-321/sfn-scrna-study
summary:
Donor-level disease classification from single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) requires strict donor-aware cross-validation: naive pipelines that split cells randomly conflate training and test donors, inflating reported performance through pseudoreplication. We present a donor-aware benchmark evaluating three feature representations across two independent IBD cohorts: centered log-ratio (CLR) transformed cell-type composition, GatedStructuralCFN dependency embeddings, and scVI variational autoencoder latent embeddings. The cohorts are the SCP259 ulcerative colitis atlas (UC vs. Healthy, n=30 donors, 51 cell types) and the Kong 2023 Crohn's disease atlas (CD vs. Healthy, n=71 donors, 55-68 cell types across three intestinal regions).
  Compartment-stratified CLR composition achieves AUROC 0.956 +/- 0.061 on SCP259; GatedStructuralCFN on the same features achieves 0.978 +/- 0.050. In the Kong cohort, CFN achieves its best performance in the colon region (0.960 +/- 0.055 after feature filtering), exceeding linear CLR (0.900 +/- 0.100), while terminal ileum classification is dominated by linear models (CatBoost CLR 0.967 +/- 0.075 vs. CFN 0.811 +/- 0.164). Cross-dataset transfer (CD->UC, four shared cell types) achieves AUC 0.833 with XGBoost CLR; the reverse direction performs at chance. CFN edge stability analysis shows that compartment-wise composition eliminates spurious unit-sum-induced instability present in global composition (Jaccard 0.026 vs. top-20 recurrence 1.0). CFN shows a consistent numerical advantage over linear models in the colon region of CD (AUROC 0.960 vs. 0.900), though no inter-method comparison reached statistical significance at n<=34 donors per region. Compartment-aware feature construction is critical for both classification performance and structural interpretability. Code: https://github.com/Jonathan-321/sfn-scrna-study

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks donor-aware classification methods for scRNA-seq disease diagnosis, showing that compartment-stratified CLR composition and GatedStructuralCFN embeddings outperform naive pipelines, though no method achieves statistically significant superiority at small sample sizes.
AI.motivation: To address the pseudoreplication problem in scRNA-seq disease classification where naive cell-random splitting inflates performance by conflating training/test donors, and to establish proper donor-aware benchmark comparisons across feature representations.
AI.method: Donor-aware benchmark comparing CLR-transformed cell-type composition, GatedStructuralCFN dependency embeddings, and scVI latent embeddings across two independent IBD cohorts (SCP259 ulcerative colitis, Kong 2023 Crohn's disease), with compartment-stratified feature construction and cross-dataset transfer evaluation.
AI.result: CLR composition achieves AUROC 0.956 on SCP259; CFN achieves 0.978. In Crohn's disease, CFN excels in colon (0.960) while linear models dominate terminal ileum (0.967). Cross-dataset transfer (CD→UC) achieves 0.833 with XGBoost CLR. CFN edge stability analysis shows compartment-wise composition eliminates spurious instability (Jaccard 0.026 vs. top-20 recurrence 1.0). No inter-method comparison reached statistical significance.
AI.conclusion: Compartment-aware feature construction is critical for both classification performance and structural interpretability in donor-level scRNA-seq disease classification, though larger donor cohorts are needed to establish statistical significance between methods.

title: A-CODE: Fully Atomic Protein Co-Design with Unified Multimodal Diffusion
summary: We present A-CODE, a fully atomic unified one-stage protein co-design model that simultaneously refines discrete atom types and continuous atom coordinates. Unlike predominant two-stage methods that cascade structure design with amino acid-level sequence design, our approach is fully atomic within a unified multimodal diffusion framework, in which residue identities are inferred solely from atom-level predictions. Built upon the powerful all-atom architecture, A-CODE achieves superior designability for unconditional protein generation, outperforming all existing one-stage and two-stage design models. For binder design, A-CODE rivals and even outperforms existing state-of-the-art two-stage design models and, compared with the existing one-stage co-design model, achieves a drastic tenfold improvement in success rate on hard tasks. The inherent flexibility of our atomic formulation enables, for the first time, seamless adaptation to non-canonical amino acid (ncAA) modeling. Our fully atomic framework establishes a new, versatile foundation for all-atom generative modeling that can be naturally extended to complex biomolecular systems.

AI.tldr: A-CODE is a fully atomic one-stage protein co-design model using a unified multimodal diffusion framework that simultaneously refines atom types and coordinates, outperforming existing methods in unconditional generation and binder design, and enabling non-canonical amino acid modeling for the first time.
AI.motivation: Existing two-stage protein design methods cascade structure design with amino acid-level sequence design, lacking atomic-level precision. One-stage co-design models also operate at residue level. The authors aim to create a fully atomic framework that simultaneously handles atom types and coordinates for more accurate and flexible protein design.
AI.method: A-CODE employs a unified multimodal diffusion framework that jointly refines discrete atom types and continuous atom coordinates in a single stage. It uses an all-atom architecture within a diffusion process, where residue identities are inferred purely from atom-level predictions, eliminating the need for separate sequence design modules.
AI.result: A-CODE achieves superior designability in unconditional protein generation, outperforming all existing one-stage and two-stage models. For binder design, it rivals/outperforms state-of-the-art two-stage models and achieves a tenfold improvement in success rate on hard tasks compared to existing one-stage models. It also demonstrates first-ever capability for non-canonical amino acid (ncAA) modeling.
AI.conclusion: The fully atomic framework establishes a new versatile foundation for all-atom generative modeling of proteins, with natural extensibility to complex biomolecular systems beyond canonical proteins.

title: Connecting IBD tracts and runs of homozygosity: A coalescent framework for inferring effective population size
summary: Identity by descent (IBD) tracts and runs of homozygosity (ROH) are related concepts that refer to the autozygosity in chromosome segments. However the formal relationship between their length distributions remains to be established. Here we present a coalescent framework that unifies these two concepts within a single analytical development. Starting from a Wright-Fisher model, we derive closed-form probability density functions for IBD tract lengths and extend these to the observable distribution of ROH lengths. This is achieved by explicitly modelling the displacement of ROH limits from true recombination breakpoints to the nearest heterozygous marker site. Mutation, gene conversion, finite marker density, and variable marker heterozygosity are incorporated as parameters in the theory that link IBD tracts to ROH. We show that the chromosome segment homozygosity (CSH) statistic emerges as a special case. This enables demographic information from IBD tracts and ROHs to be combined into a framework for inferring effective population size. Finally, we incorporate the quantitative genetic theory of background selection into the IBD length distribution, to show how selection introduces a systematic upward bias in apparent tract lengths. This demonstrates that no single Ne value can account for the entire IBD length distribution under selection. The application of this theory to the detection of selection signatures in the genome is illustrated using the example of the local selective sweep associated with lactase persistence in human populations.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a unified coalescent framework linking IBD tracts and ROH length distributions, deriving closed-form probability densities from a Wright-Fisher model, incorporating mutation, gene conversion, marker density, and selection effects, with application to demographic inference and selection detection (e.g., lactase persistence).
AI.motivation: To establish a formal theoretical relationship between IBD tract and ROH length distributions, which are related but previously lacked a unified analytical foundation, and to enable combined demographic inference and selection detection.
AI.method: Coalescent framework derived from Wright-Fisher model; closed-form PDFs for IBD tract lengths; modeling ROH limit displacement from recombination breakpoints to nearest heterozygous markers; incorporating mutation, gene conversion, finite marker density, variable heterozygosity; integrating background selection theory into IBD length distribution.
AI.result: Closed-form probability density functions for IBD and ROH lengths; CSH statistic as a special case; demonstration that selection introduces upward bias in tract lengths (no single Ne explains the full distribution); application to lactase persistence selection signature.
AI.conclusion: IBD and ROH can be unified in a single analytical coalescent framework incorporating multiple biological and technical parameters; this enables combined demographic inference and reveals selection biases in tract length distributions.

title: Expanding functional protein sequence space using high entropy generative models
summary: Boltzmann Machines trained on evolutionary sequence data have emerged as a powerful paradigm for the data-driven design of artificial proteins. However, the relationship between model architecture, specifically parameter density, and experimental performance remains poorly understood. Here, we investigate this relationship using the Chorismate Mutase enzyme family as a model system. We compare standard fully connected Boltzmann Machines for Direct Coupling Analysis (bmDCA) with sparse models generated via progressive edge activation (eaDCA) and edge decimation (edDCA). We identify a maximum-entropy model (meDCA) along the decimation trajectory that represents an optimal balance between constraint satisfaction and the flexibility of the probability distribution. We synthesized and tested artificial sequences from all models using an in vivo complementation assay, finding that all architectures, regardless of sparsity, generate functional enzymes with high success rates, even at significant divergence from natural sequences. Despite this functional equivalence, we demonstrate that the meDCA model samples a viable sequence space that is more than fifteen orders of magnitude larger than its low-entropy counterparts. Furthermore, comparative analyses reveal that high-entropy models systematically minimize overfitting and better capture the local neutral spaces surrounding natural proteins. These findings suggest that while various models satisfying coevolutionary statistics can generate functional sequences, high-entropy Boltzmann Machines provide a superior representation of the underlying evolutionary fitness landscape.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how the sparsity of Boltzmann Machine models (trained on evolutionary sequence data) affects their ability to design functional artificial proteins. Using the Chorismate Mutase enzyme family, the authors compare dense (bmDCA), sparse (eaDCA/edDCA), and a maximum-entropy model (meDCA). All models generate functional enzymes with high success rates, but the high-entropy meDCA model samples a vastly larger viable sequence space and better captures the local neutral space around natural proteins, suggesting it provides a superior representation of the evolutionary fitness landscape.
AI.motivation: To understand the relationship between model architecture (specifically parameter density/sparsity) and experimental performance in Boltzmann Machines trained on evolutionary sequence data for protein design, as this relationship remains poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors use Chorismate Mutase enzyme family as a model system. They compare standard fully connected Boltzmann Machines (bmDCA) with sparse models generated via progressive edge activation (eaDCA) and edge decimation (edDCA). They identify a maximum-entropy model (meDCA) along the decimation trajectory. Artificial sequences from all models were synthesized and tested using an in vivo complementation assay.
AI.result: All architectures, regardless of sparsity, generated functional enzymes with high success rates. However, the meDCA model sampled a viable sequence space more than 15 orders of magnitude larger than low-entropy counterparts. High-entropy models systematically minimize overfitting and better capture local neutral spaces surrounding natural proteins.
AI.conclusion: While various models satisfying coevolutionary statistics can generate functional sequences, high-entropy Boltzmann Machines provide a superior representation of the underlying evolutionary fitness landscape.

title: Robust chemotaxis beyond sensing limits: signal, noise, and strategy
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 2 appendices. This is the accepted manuscript version; the final published version will appear in Physical Biology (IOP Publishing) and will be available under a CC BY license
summary: Bacterial chemotaxis has long been viewed as operating near the physical limits of sensing, as originally articulated by Berg and Purcell. Recent information-theoretic analyses challenge this view, suggesting that Escherichia coli uses only a small fraction of the information available in ligand arrival statistics to bias its motion. How should such low information efficiency be interpreted at the level of behavior? Here, I argue that chemotactic performance is shaped not only by information transmission and noise, but by the strategy of movement itself. Using simple scaling arguments and minimal models, I show how run-and-tumble chemotaxis can remain robust to noise through symmetry and temporal averaging, even when internal information processing is inefficient. Comparing bacterial and eukaryotic chemotaxis highlights how different sensing strategies convert physical limits into observable behavior. These considerations suggest that low information efficiency need not imply poor performance, but may instead reflect an evolved balance between robustness, simplicity, and function.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that low information efficiency in bacterial chemotaxis does not imply poor performance; instead, robustness, symmetry, and temporal averaging allow run-and-tumble strategies to function well despite inefficient internal information processing.
AI.motivation: To resolve the paradox that E. coli uses only a small fraction of available chemical information to bias its motion, yet performs robust chemotaxis, by exploring behavioral-level interpretations beyond information-theoretic limits.
AI.method: Scaling arguments and minimal models are used to examine how run-and-tumble chemotaxis achieves noise robustness through symmetry and temporal averaging; bacterial and eukaryotic chemotaxis are compared.
AI.result: Low information efficiency does not necessarily indicate poor chemotactic performance; it may reflect an evolved trade-off between robustness, simplicity, and function.
AI.conclusion: Chemotactic performance is shaped not only by information transmission and noise but critically by the movement strategy itself, suggesting that efficiency metrics should be interpreted in the context of behavioral robustness.

title: AgenticPosesRanker: An Agentic AI Framework for Physically Grounded Ranking of Protein-Ligand Docking Poses
summary: Scoring functions remain the principal bottleneck in molecular docking: they routinely fail to rank near-native poses above decoys, and their composite single-score design obscures the physicochemical basis of each ranking error. We present AgenticPosesRanker, an agentic AI framework that combines six deterministic, physically grounded analysis tools (interaction fingerprinting, solvent-accessible burial, conformational strain, steric-clash detection, unsatisfied-polar-atom penalty, and chemical-identity extraction) with large-language-model (GPT-5) chain-of-thought reasoning to evaluate and rank docking poses. On a curated benchmark of ten protein-ligand systems (162 poses) balanced by construction between Smina scoring-function successes and failures, the agent achieved 50.0% best-pose accuracy, matching the design-fixed Smina baseline of 50.0% and significantly exceeding a 7.7% uniformly random baseline (p < 0.001, one-sided exact binomial test). The balanced-benchmark accuracy decomposes symmetrically: the agent retained 80% (4/5) of the Smina-success systems and recovered 20% (1/5) of the Smina-failure systems, so the aggregate 50% reflects one regression offset by one recovery rather than any net improvement over the Smina reference. Decision-attribution analysis showed high alignment between the agent's self-reported tool weights and objective metric separations of the selected pose (median \r{ho} = +0.83), consistent across correct and incorrect outcomes, localising the performance ceiling to tool-suite coverage rather than reasoning inconsistency. These results establish a methodological template for evaluating agentic AI against objective ground truth in the natural sciences and position the framework as an interpretable curation layer for late-stage pose refinement in structure-based drug design.

AI.tldr: AgenticPosesRanker is an agentic AI framework using GPT-5 chain-of-thought reasoning with six physics-based analysis tools to rank docking poses, achieving 50% accuracy (matching Smina baseline) on a balanced benchmark of 162 poses. The framework shows high decision-attribution consistency but is limited by tool-suite coverage rather than reasoning quality.
AI.motivation: Scoring functions in molecular docking routinely fail to rank near-native poses above decoys, and their composite single-score design obscures the physicochemical basis of ranking errors.
AI.method: Combines six deterministic physics-based analysis tools (interaction fingerprinting, solvent-accessible burial, conformational strain, steric-clash detection, unsatisfied-polar-atom penalty, chemical-identity extraction) with GPT-5 chain-of-thought reasoning to evaluate and rank docking poses.
AI.result: On a curated benchmark of ten protein-ligand systems (162 poses), the agent achieved 50.0% best-pose accuracy, matching Smina baseline and significantly exceeding 7.7% random baseline (p<0.001). Retained 80% of Smina successes and recovered 20% of Smina failures.
AI.conclusion: The performance ceiling is localized to tool-suite coverage rather than reasoning inconsistency. Establishes a methodological template for evaluating agentic AI against objective ground truth, positioning it as an interpretable curation layer for late-stage pose refinement in drug design.

title: Statistical analysis of virion-cell interactions mediated by peptide nanofibrils and peptide amphiphiles using STEM tomography
summary: Peptide nanofibrils (PNFs) and peptide amphiphiles (PAs) are promising tools for enhancing viral transduction and gene transfer. However, quantitative insight into how their supramolecular architecture governs virion-cell interactions is limited. Here, we introduce a framework for the acquisition, processing, and statistical analysis of scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) tomograms to objectively quantify peptide-virion-cell interactions. Using four transduction-enhancing peptides (D4, Vectofusin-1, palmitic acid-PA (pal-PA), and eicosapentaenoic-PA (eic-PA)), peptide aggregate morphology, interfacial contact areas, and the spatial organization of virions with respect to peptides and cells were analyzed using advanced geometric descriptors. All peptides efficiently captured virions, resulting in few free virions, but they differ in how strictly virions were spatially confined near the cell surface. These differences reflect alternative spatial organization strategies, which are likely crucial factors influencing transduction-enhancing efficacy. Our approach provides a novel, generalizable method to evaluate infection-enhancing nanomaterials and guides the rational design of next-generation peptide assemblies for therapeutic viral delivery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantitative STEM tomography framework to analyze how different peptide nanofibrils and peptide amphiphiles interact with virions and cells, revealing distinct spatial organization strategies that explain differences in transduction-enhancing efficacy.
AI.motivation: Quantitative insight into how supramolecular architecture of peptide nanofibrils and amphiphiles governs virion-cell interactions is limited, despite their promise for enhancing viral transduction and gene transfer.
AI.method: A framework for acquisition, processing, and statistical analysis of STEM tomograms was developed, using geometric descriptors to analyze peptide aggregate morphology, interfacial contact areas, and spatial organization of virions relative to peptides and cells across four transduction-enhancing peptides (D4, Vectofusin-1, pal-PA, eic-PA).
AI.result: All peptides efficiently captured virions (few free virions), but they exhibited different strategies for spatial confinement of virions near the cell surface, reflecting alternative spatial organization patterns.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a generalizable method to evaluate infection-enhancing nanomaterials and can guide rational design of next-generation peptide assemblies for therapeutic viral delivery.

title: Non-Monotone Response Modules and Cascades from the EML Operator for Reduced Models of Biological Dynamics
comment: Main text: 9 pages, 4 figures. Appendix is 5 pages
summary: Standard saturating response functions, such as the Hill function, are monotone and therefore cannot represent recruitment-induced overshoot or adaptive transients with a single block. Reproducing such non-monotone responses from saturating primitives requires at least a difference of two blocks with opposing amplitudes, doubling the static-block parameter count. Here, building on a recent mathematical result that a single binary operator, EML, generates all standard elementary functions, we use EML as a structured grammar for reduced nonlinear ODEs. This yields an activation-suppression module that captures overshoot directly. We validate the framework in three settings. First, on PKA-R relocalization data, the EML grammar discovers a reduced surrogate consistent with established mechanistic biology. Second, on Rho-GTPase recruitment data, an exhaustive search over EML expression trees selects the same compositional form across all four perturbation-response traces. Third, a 50-state simulated network is compressed by an EML cascade acting as a fixed temporal basis. Thus we demonstrate the power and potential of EML for reduced models of biological dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EML (a binary operator) as a grammar for building reduced nonlinear ODE models that can capture non-monotone biological responses (like overshoot and adaptive transients) using a single activation-suppression module, validated on PKA-R, Rho-GTPase, and simulated network data.
AI.motivation: Standard saturating response functions (e.g., Hill function) are monotone and cannot represent non-monotone behaviors like overshoot or adaptive transients without using two blocks, doubling parameter count. The authors aim to find a more parsimonious framework.
AI.method: The authors leverage the EML binary operator (which generates all standard elementary functions) as a structured grammar for constructing reduced nonlinear ODEs. This yields an activation-suppression module that captures overshoot directly with a single block.
AI.result: 1) On PKA-R relocalization data, EML discovered a reduced surrogate consistent with known biology. 2) On Rho-GTPase data, exhaustive search over EML expression trees selected the same compositional form across all four perturbation-response traces. 3) A 50-state simulated network was compressed by an EML cascade acting as a fixed temporal basis.
AI.conclusion: The EML framework demonstrates significant power and potential for building reduced models of biological dynamics, enabling parsimonious representation of complex non-monotone responses.

title: Approaching human parity in the quality of automated organoid image segmentation
summary:
Organoids are complex, three dimensional, self-organizing cell cultures which manifest organ-like features and represent a powerful platform for studying human disease and developing treatment options. Organoid development is characterized by dynamic morphological and cellular organization, which mimic some aspects of organ development. To study these rapid changes over the course of organoid development, advanced imaging and analytical tools are critical to accurately monitor the trajectory of organoid growth and investigate disease processes.
  In this work, we focus on computer vision and machine learning techniques to automatically measure the size and shape of developing spheroids derived from pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), which are typically the starting material for generating organoid cultures. To facilitate this task, we introduce a composite method that combines the Segment Anything Model (SAM), a general-purpose foundation model, with an existing domain-specific tool. This composite method is evaluated together with several existing tools by testing them on organoid image data and comparing with the results of manual image segmentation. We find that no single existing tool is able to segment the test images with sufficient accuracy across all test conditions, but the newly introduced composite method produces consistent and accurate results for all but a very small fraction of the most challenging images. Finally, we compare the accuracy of this method to the variability between manual segmentations by independent annotators (inter-observer variability) and find that by one measure it performs at the level of inter-observer variability and by others it performs very close to it.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a composite computer vision method combining SAM with a domain-specific tool to automatically measure size and shape of iPSC-derived spheroids, achieving accuracy close to or at the level of inter-observer variability.
AI.motivation: Organoids are powerful platforms for studying disease and treatments, but their dynamic morphological development requires advanced imaging and analytical tools that can accurately and automatically track growth trajectories, which current existing tools fail to do consistently.
AI.method: A composite method combining the Segment Anything Model (SAM), a general-purpose foundation model, with an existing domain-specific tool for segmentation of spheroid images.
AI.result: No single existing tool sufficiently segmented test images across all conditions, but the composite method produced consistent and accurate results for nearly all images, performing at or very close to the level of inter-observer variability.
AI.conclusion: The composite approach effectively bridges the gap between general-purpose AI models and domain-specific tools, offering reliable automated segmentation for organoid research.

title: Dynamic Vine Copulas: Detecting and Quantifying Time-Varying Higher-Order Interactions
summary: Time varying dependence is often modeled through dynamic correlations or Gaussian graphical models, yet many multivariate systems change through tail behavior, asymmetry, or conditional structure while correlations change little. We introduce Dynamic Vine Copulas (DVC), a temporal vine copula framework for estimating and diagnosing sequence wide non-Gaussian dependence. DVC keeps a chosen vine factorization fixed for comparability, can use C-, D-, or R-vines, and couples pair copula states across time through smooth parameter trajectories or temporally regularized family switching paths. Its central diagnostic contrasts held-out scores from a full vine and its matched 1-truncated counterpart, separating flexible first-tree pairwise evidence from higher-tree conditional evidence. At the population level, under a correct fixed vine and simplifying assumption, this contrast is the higher-tree term of a vine total correlation decomposition; in finite samples, it is a predictive diagnostic. Across controlled benchmarks, DVC detects Student-t tail degree changes, Clayton-to-Gumbel switches, and recurrent conditional interaction episodes that Gaussian dynamic baselines miss or conflate. The higher-tree score stays near zero in pairwise only regimes but rises selectively during conditional interaction regimes. On Allen Visual Behavior Neuropixels data, DVC identifies a reproducible time indexed higher-tree signal that is positive across held-out splits and disappears under a decorrelated null, indicating simultaneous cross-area dependence. Together, these results show that DVC is both a flexible temporal copula model and an interpretable diagnostic for whether time varying dependence changes are pairwise or conditional.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Dynamic Vine Copulas (DVC), a temporal vine copula framework that models non-Gaussian time-varying dependence beyond simple correlations. DVC keeps a fixed vine structure, allows smooth or switching pair-copula dynamics, and introduces a diagnostic contrast between full and 1-truncated vines to distinguish pairwise from conditional dependence changes. Experiments show DVC detects tail dependence shifts, family switches, and conditional interactions missed by Gaussian models, and reveals reproducible higher-tree signals in neural data.
AI.motivation: Many multivariate systems exhibit time-varying dependence in tail behavior, asymmetry, or conditional structure that correlation-based or Gaussian graphical models fail to capture. The authors aim to provide a flexible framework that both models and diagnoses whether dependence changes are purely pairwise or also involve higher-order conditional interactions.
AI.method: DVC extends vine copulas to temporal settings by keeping a fixed vine factorization, supporting C-, D-, and R-vines, and modeling pair-copula parameters as smooth trajectories or regularized family-switching paths. Key diagnostic: contrast held-out scores from full vine vs. its 1-truncated version — at population level, this equals the higher-tree term of vine total correlation decomposition; in finite samples, it's a predictive diagnostic for detecting conditional vs. pairwise dependence changes.
AI.result: DVC detects Student-t tail degree changes, Clayton-to-Gumbel switches, and recurrent conditional interaction episodes — patterns missed by Gaussian dynamic baselines. Higher-tree diagnostic scores stay near zero under pairwise-only regimes but rise selectively during conditional interaction regimes. On Allen Brain Neuropixels data, DVC finds reproducible time-indexed higher-tree signals that vanish under decorrelated null, indicating genuine cross-area conditional dependence.
AI.conclusion: DVC is both a flexible temporal copula model for non-Gaussian time-varying dependence and an interpretable diagnostic tool for distinguishing whether dependence changes are pairwise or involve higher-order conditional structure.

title: The Incommensurability Principle in Biological Transport
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure. Paper IV in a series on branching transport networks; see also arXiv:2603.13687 (Paper I), arXiv:2603.14691 (Paper II), and arXiv:2604.10476 (Paper III). Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20030962
summary:
Biological vascular networks exhibit branching exponents ($α^* \approx 2.72$) conserved across developmental stages and observed in multiple mammalian species [Kassab et al. (1993), Zamir (1999)], despite vast metabolic and anatomical variation. We prove this universality is a mathematical necessity arising from the physical incommensurability of optimization constraints. We establish three theorems.
  (1) No-Go Theorem: Local optimization combining extensive metabolic costs with dimensionless wave-reflection penalties requires a coupling parameter varying by $10^2$--$10^3$ across the hierarchy, precluding universal exponents.
  (2) Metabolic Gauge Invariance: The unique dimensionless cost functional consistent with scale invariance and thermodynamic linearity is the fractional metabolic excess; alternative penalties (logarithmic measures) fail empirical validation.
  (3) Architectural Invariance: The minimax duty cycle $η^*$ is an exact invariant of the allometric class $\mathcal{A}(G,p,α_w)$, orthogonal to absolute metabolic scales -- explaining developmental stability. The minimax emerges as the unique attractor for networks optimizing physically incommensurable costs, unifying previous single-mechanism results as degenerate boundary cases.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the conserved branching exponent (α* ≈ 2.72) in biological vascular networks is a mathematical necessity arising from the incommensurability of optimization constraints, not merely a biological observation. Three theorems establish that local optimization fails to produce universal exponents, the unique scale-invariant cost functional is the fractional metabolic excess, and the minimax duty cycle is an exact invariant explaining developmental stability.
AI.motivation: To explain why branching exponents in vascular networks are universally conserved (α* ≈ 2.72) across species and developmental stages despite vast metabolic and anatomical variation, and to prove this universality is a mathematical necessity.
AI.method: Three theorems are established: (1) No-Go Theorem - showing local optimization with coupled metabolic and wave-reflection costs requires varying coupling parameters, precluding universal exponents; (2) Metabolic Gauge Invariance - identifying the fractional metabolic excess as the unique dimensionless cost functional consistent with scale invariance and thermodynamic linearity; (3) Architectural Invariance - demonstrating the minimax duty cycle η* is an exact invariant of the allometric class.
AI.result: The conserved branching exponent α* ≈ 2.72 is proven to be a mathematical necessity from physical incommensurability of optimization constraints, not merely a biological observation. The minimax duty cycle emerges as the unique attractor for networks optimizing physically incommensurable costs.
AI.conclusion: The universality of branching exponents is a mathematical necessity arising from incommensurable optimization constraints, with the minimax duty cycle unifying previous single-mechanism results as degenerate boundary cases. This explains developmental stability and cross-species conservation.

title: Cusped singularities organize mixed-mode oscillations in mutually inhibitory slow-fast systems
summary: Mutual inhibition is a common motif in neural systems. Here, we establish that cusped singularities - folded singularities located at cusp points of critical manifolds - provide a universal organizing mechanism for mixed-mode oscillations (MMOs) in coupled slow-fast systems with mutual inhibition. We show that the geometric setup of these systems generically satisfies the conditions required by established geometric singular perturbation theory and blow-up methods, guaranteeing that such cusped singularities yield small-amplitude oscillations (SAOs). MMOs appear from the SAOs combined with an appropriate return mechanism. Further, we show that the geometric presence of a cusped singularity is strictly related to occurrence of a nearby singular Hopf bifurcation. We demonstrate the efficacy of this framework in two distinct neuronal models: the Curtu rate model of mutually inhibitory neural populations and coupled Morris-Lecar neurons with synaptic inhibition. In both cases, pushing the full system equilibrium near the cusped singularity triggers SAOs as the system passes near the cusp and approaches a full-system saddle-focus related to the singular Hopf bifurcation. Large-amplitude oscillations appear as the system spirals away from the saddle-focus, leading to MMOs, which may exhibit distinctive alternating patterns, in contrast to standard saddle-node induced MMOs. Our results establish cusped singularities as a generic, biologically relevant mechanism for complex oscillatory dynamics in inhibitory neural networks as well as for other inhibitory slow-fast systems.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that cusped singularities (folded singularities at cusp points of critical manifolds) serve as a universal mechanism for generating mixed-mode oscillations (MMOs) in coupled slow-fast systems with mutual inhibition, demonstrated in two neuronal models.
AI.motivation: Mutual inhibition is a common motif in neural systems, but the underlying mechanisms for complex oscillatory patterns like MMOs in such systems were not fully understood. The authors aim to establish a universal geometric organizing principle.
AI.method: The authors apply geometric singular perturbation theory and blow-up methods to show that cusped singularities generically produce small-amplitude oscillations (SAOs). When combined with a return mechanism, MMOs emerge. They validate the framework in the Curtu rate model and coupled Morris-Lecar neurons.
AI.result: Cusped singularities are strictly related to nearby singular Hopf bifurcations. Pushing the equilibrium near the cusp triggers SAOs, and large-amplitude oscillations appear as the system spirals away from a saddle-focus, producing MMOs with distinctive alternating patterns different from standard saddle-node induced MMOs.
AI.conclusion: Cusped singularities provide a generic, biologically relevant mechanism for complex oscillatory dynamics in inhibitory neural networks and other inhibitory slow-fast systems.

title: Graph Neural Network based Hierarchy-Aware Embeddings of Knowledge Graphs: Applications to Yeast Phenotype Prediction
summary:
We present a method for finding hierarchy-aware embeddings of knowledge graphs (KGs) using graph neural networks (GNNs) enriched with a semantic loss derived from underlying ontologies. This method yields embeddings that better reflect domain knowledge. To demonstrate their utility, we predict and interpret the effects of gene deletions in the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae and learn box embeddings for KGs in the absence of a prediction task. We further show how box embeddings can serve as the basis for evaluating KG revisions.
  Our yeast KG is constructed from community databases and ontology terms. Low-dimensional box embeddings combined with GNNs are used to predict cell growth for double gene knockouts. Over 10-fold cross validation, these predictions have a mean $R^2$~score~of~0.360, significantly higher than baseline comparisons, demonstrating that high-level qualitative knowledge is informative about experimental outcomes. Incorporating semantic loss terms in the training of the models improves their predictive performance ($R^2$=0.377) by aligning embeddings with ontology structure. This shows that class hierarchies from ontologies can be exploited for quantitative prediction. We also test the trained models on triple gene knockouts, showing they generalise to data beyond those seen in training.
  Additionally, by identifying co-occurring relations in the yeast KG important for the cell-growth predictions, we construct hypotheses about interacting traits in yeast. A biological experiment validates one such finding, revealing an association between inositol utilisation and osmotic stress resistance, highlighting the model's potential to guide biological discovery.

AI.tldr: A method using GNNs with ontology-based semantic loss produces hierarchy-aware KG embeddings. Applied to yeast gene deletion prediction (R²=0.377) and box embeddings for KG revision, revealing novel biological associations like inositol-utilization and osmotic stress resistance.
AI.motivation: To incorporate high-level ontology knowledge into KG embeddings for improved prediction and interpretability, demonstrating that qualitative domain knowledge can enhance quantitative experimental outcome predictions.
AI.method: Constructs a yeast KG from community databases and ontologies; uses GNNs with box embeddings and a semantic loss term derived from ontology hierarchies to predict cell growth for gene knockouts (double and triple).
AI.result: Double knockout predictions achieve R²=0.360 (baseline-beating); adding semantic loss improves to R²=0.377. Models generalize to triple knockouts. Co-occurring relation analysis generates testable hypotheses; one (inositol utilization ↔ osmotic stress resistance) was experimentally validated.
AI.conclusion: Ontology-aware semantic loss meaningfully improves KG embedding quality for both prediction and KG revision tasks, and the approach can guide biological discovery by generating interpretable hypotheses.

title: Inferring Phylogenetic Networks from Allowed and Forbidden LCA-Constraints
summary: Phylogenetic networks provide a framework for representing evolutionary histories involving reticulate events such as hybridization or horizontal gene transfer. A central problem is to infer such networks from local structural information. In this paper, we study network inference from least common ancestor (LCA) constraints, which specify relative ancestral relationships between pairs of taxa. While previous work has characterized when a set of required LCA constraints can be realized by a phylogenetic network, practical applications may also involve constraints that must be explicitly avoided, for example due to biological prior knowledge. We therefore consider the realization problem for pairs $(R,F)$, where $R$ is a set of allowed or, equivalently, required LCA-constraints and $F$ is a set of forbidden ones. Since there are several natural ways to formalize what it means for a network to avoid a forbidden LCA-constraint, we study three such variants. For each of them, we characterize exactly when there exists a phylogenetic network that realizes all constraints in $R$ while avoiding all constraints in $F$ in the respective sense. Based on these characterizations, we derive polynomial-time algorithms that decide the existence of such networks and construct one whenever it exists.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how to infer phylogenetic networks from both required and forbidden least common ancestor (LCA) constraints, providing polynomial-time algorithms for three variants of the realization problem.
AI.motivation: While previous work could realize required LCA constraints into phylogenetic networks, practical applications also involve constraints that must be avoided (e.g., due to biological prior knowledge), but this combined problem had not been addressed.
AI.method: The authors formalize three natural variants of what it means to avoid a forbidden LCA-constraint, characterize exactly when a phylogenetic network exists that satisfies all required constraints while avoiding all forbidden ones under each variant, and derive polynomial-time algorithms for decision and construction.
AI.result: Complete characterizations for three variants of avoiding forbidden LCA constraints, along with polynomial-time algorithms that decide existence and construct networks when possible.
AI.conclusion: The work extends phylogenetic network inference to handle both required and forbidden LCA constraints, providing efficient algorithmic solutions with practical applicability.

title: Catastrophe-dispersion models in random and varying environments across generations
summary: We study a class of branching processes in which the offspring distribution is not specified directly but is induced by a cycle of internal colony growth, catastrophic reduction and structured dispersal. The parameters governing growth, survival and dispersal are allowed to vary deterministically or randomly from one generation to the next, giving rise to branching processes in varying and random environments with implicitly defined offspring laws. We show that survival and extinction are governed entirely by the associated log-mean process, exactly as in the classical theory. The paper treats four qualitatively different dispersal mechanisms and establishes a universal ordering of the induced offspring means. For Poissonian growth with binomial survival, explicit thresholds are obtained that determine extinction or survival uniformly over all four mechanisms. A series of ecologically motivated examples with Yule-Simon growth illustrates the versatility of the framework.

AI.tldr: This paper studies branching processes where offspring distributions are implicitly defined by internal colony growth, catastrophic reduction, and structured dispersal, showing that survival/extinction is governed by the log-mean process as in classical theory, with explicit thresholds for Poissonian growth with binomial survival.
AI.motivation: To generalize branching process theory to cases where offspring distributions are not directly specified but emerge from ecological mechanisms (growth, catastrophic reduction, dispersal), allowing parameters to vary deterministically or randomly across generations.
AI.method: The authors analyze four qualitatively different dispersal mechanisms, establish a universal ordering of induced offspring means, and derive explicit extinction/survival thresholds for Poissonian growth with binomial survival, with Yule-Simon growth examples.
AI.result: Survival and extinction are entirely governed by the associated log-mean process, mirroring classical branching process theory. A universal ordering of offspring means across the four dispersal mechanisms is established.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully extends branching process theory to implicitly defined offspring distributions in varying/random environments, with ecologically motivated examples demonstrating versatility.
